Title: A pipeline for identification of causal mutations in barley identifies Xantha-j as the chlorophyll synthase gene

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: UbiA family prenyltransferase; 9 transmembrane helices; chloroplast transit peptide
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Bonus; Foma; Quench-cross F2 mapping populations
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: xan-j mRNA detected in barley seedling leaves; increased in clomazone-treated white seedlings; reduced significantly in xan-j.59 mutant
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Seedling leaves; analyzed in yellow xantha seedlings and clomazone-treated seedlings
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Chlorophyllide; geranylgeranyl pyrophosphate (GGPP) and/or phytyl pyrophosphate (PhyPP)
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes addition of a phytol/geranylgeranyl-derived isoprenoid moiety to the propionate side chain of chlorophyllide in the last step of chlorophyll biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established as rate-limiting; terminal step proposed to generate regulatory/sensing signals coordinating chlorophyll and isoprenoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Photosynthetic light harvesting pigment essential for chloroplast pigmentation and seedling viability
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss-of-function xan-j mutations abolish chlorophyll accumulation causing yellow lethal seedlings; S212F allele likely disrupts prenyl-substrate binding and enzyme stability; chlorophyll synthase may sense isoprenoid availability
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Bulked segregant analysis with whole-genome sequencing; candidate mutation identification; PCR and Sanger sequencing of three alleles; immunoblotting; RT-qPCR; structural modeling with AlphaFold2 and docking; clomazone treatment; phenotype analysis under low and high light
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Low light (~100 lx); high light (~10,000 lx); clomazone treatment inhibiting chloroplastic isoprenoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Homozygous mutants are seedling lethal and chlorophyll deficient; some alleles show high-light sensitivity and necrotic/bleached tissue
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Xantha-f (XanF) showed increased protein abundance in xan-j mutants and clomazone-treated plants, but no direct physical interaction demonstrated
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Whole-genome sequencing of F2 mutant bulks; exome capture variation dataset of 815 barley accessions; NCBI SRA BioProject PRJNA1095161
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Three mutant alleles characterized: xan-j.19 frameshift near C-terminus, xan-j.59 early nonsense mutation, xan-j.64 S212F substitution in prenyl-pyrophosphate tunnel. XanJ protein was undetectable in all three mutants. Clomazone treatment also eliminated detectable XanJ protein and increased xan-j mRNA, supporting substrate-dependent protein stability. Authors suggest chlorophyll synthase acts as a GGPP/PhyPP sensor.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Xantha-j was mapped and identified as the barley chlorophyll synthase gene. 2. Three independent xan-j alleles cause chlorophyll deficiency and lethal yellow seedlings. 3. The S212F mutation likely blocks prenyl-substrate binding through steric clash in the PPP tunnel. 4. Lack of isoprenoid substrate availability via clomazone treatment also prevents XanJ protein accumulation. 5. XanJ is proposed to coordinate chlorophyll and isoprenoid biosynthesis by sensing GGPP/PhyPP availability.

Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: Magnesium protoporphyrin IX monomethyl ester cyclase
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes cyclization of magnesium protoporphyrin IX monomethyl ester in chlorophyll biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Known mutant control with chlorophyll-deficient xantha phenotype; causal nonsense mutation in cyclase gene confirmed from prior work and remapped here
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Bulked segregant analysis with whole-genome sequencing; prior gene identification cited
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Whole-genome sequencing of F2 mutant bulk; NCBI SRA BioProject PRJNA1095161
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included primarily as proof-of-principle control for the mapping pipeline; not the main mechanistic focus of the study
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. xan-l.82 mapped to the centromeric region of chromosome 3H. 2. Candidate filtering recovered the previously known magnesium protoporphyrin IX monomethyl ester cyclase gene. 3. This validated the mapping pipeline for recombination-poor regions.

Regulation_Genes.Regulator_Type: Pathway flux regulator enzyme subunit; magnesium chelatase large subunit
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: xan-f mRNA in seedling leaves was not significantly changed in xan-j mutants
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Protein abundance increased in all xan-j mutants and clomazone-treated white seedlings
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Flux of protoporphyrin IX toward chlorophyll biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Positive control point in pathway flux; protein accumulation increased post-transcriptionally in xan-j mutants and after clomazone treatment
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Entry of tetrapyrrole precursors into chlorophyll branch via magnesium chelatase reaction
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Altered XanJ function; clomazone-induced chloroplastic isoprenoid deficiency
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Implied pathway feedback coordination with terminal chlorophyll synthesis step, but not directly demonstrated
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Described as the main regulator for flux of protoporphyrin IX toward chlorophyll biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: XanF protein accumulates to higher levels in xan-j mutants and clomazone-treated seedlings, suggesting altered regulation of chlorophyll pathway flux
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: High light/low light comparisons for xan-j mutants; clomazone treatment
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: XanJ (functional relationship inferred from mutant and treatment responses)
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although central to interpretation of pathway regulation, xan-f was not the primary mapped gene in this study and was monitored mainly as a pathway regulatory marker
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. XanF is described as the main regulator of flux into chlorophyll biosynthesis. 2. XanF protein, but not xan-f mRNA, increased in all xan-j mutants. 3. XanF also increased after clomazone treatment, linking terminal-step disruption or isoprenoid shortage to upstream pathway regulation.
